Returns { id, userName, role, text, createdAt }. opts: { user?, after?, before?, role?, limit?, order? } - semanticFilter(msgs, concept, threshold?) → ScoredMessageResult[] — filter chat messages by meaning (pre-computed embeddings) - filterBySimilarity(items, concept, opts?) → {item, similarity}[] — generic semantic filter for any array - gmail.search(query, {max?}) → [{id, date, from, subject, labels, messageCount}] - gmail.messagesSearch(query, {max?, includeBody?}) → GmailMessage[] - gmail.get(messageId) → GmailMessage — { id, threadId, from, to, subject, date, body, labels, attachments } - gmail.thread(threadId, {full?}) → {id, messages} - twitter.feed(username?, page?) → XML timeline - twitter.search(query, page?, sort?) → XML results
Use Promise.all() for parallel searches. Keyword query syntax: "word word" (AND), "word OR word", "exact phrase", -word (exclude).

Michigan Liquor Control Commission (MLCC) – Manufacturer Licenses for Brewing

Michigan offers several license types relevant to brewers:

**Microbrewer License**
- Allows production of up to 60,000 barrels per year
- Can operate a taproom on the licensed premises
- Can sell beer for on-premises consumption and off-premises (packaged)
- Does NOT require food service
- Cost: $100 application fee + $400 annual fee (2025 rates)
- Must apply through MLCC eLicense portal
- Processing time: typically 60–90 days

**Brewpub License**
- For operations that are primarily food service restaurants that also brew beer
- Production capped at 18,000 barrels/year
- Requires substantial food menu
- Tied to a specific retail food service license

**Key note:** On-site brewing for taproom sales = Microbrewer license is the standard path. Brewpub is used when the food operation is the primary business driver.

**Federal requirement:** All on-site brewers must also obtain a Brewer's Notice from the TTB (Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau) before beginning production — this is a federal permit, separate from state licensing, and is free but can take 60+ days.

Additional Michigan requirements:
- MLCC background check for all owners
- Proof of premises (lease or ownership)
- Local municipality approval/zoning compliance sign-off required before MLCC will issue license

Source: michigan.gov/mlcc (accessed Nov 2025)

Traverse City / Grand Traverse County Business Licensing – Brewery/Taproom

Traverse City operates under City of Traverse City zoning ordinances (separate from Grand Traverse County for unincorporated areas).

**Local requirements for a taproom in Traverse City city limits:**
- City of TC business license (annual, ~$75–$150 depending on category)
- Zoning approval: Taprooms are permitted in certain commercial/mixed-use zones (B-2, B-3, industrial transition zones common in TC). Requires confirmation that the specific address is zoned correctly for food/beverage manufacturing + retail.
- Building permit and Certificate of Occupancy: if space was previously a different use (e.g., restaurant to brewery), a change-of-use building permit may be required even if the physical build-out is minimal
- Grand Traverse County Health Department: food service license required if serving any food items; triggers inspection process
- Michigan Department of Agriculture & Rural Development (MDARD): may also be relevant if producing packaged goods

**Note on prior restaurant spaces:** A space previously permitted as a restaurant may have favorable infrastructure (grease traps, ventilation, ADA compliance) but the certificate of occupancy will still need to reflect the new use classification. Brewery/taproom is a different use category than restaurant in TC zoning code.

Contacts:
- City of TC Planning: (231) 922-4480
- GT County Health Dept: (231) 995-6112

Source: traversecitymi.gov, traversecountymi.gov (accessed Nov 2025)

That landlord texting you twice a week? That's not a red flag about him — that's information about your negotiating position. He's been eating vacancy costs for 5+ years. He needs you more than you need him right now. **Do not match his urgency.** Let him wait.

**How TI works:**

The landlord gives you money (or credits against rent) to improve the space. In return:

- **Yes, the improvements stay with the building.** New plumbing, flooring, electrical work, HVAC mods — that becomes part of his property. That's the core tradeoff. You're upgrading his building, he's funding it.
- **Your brewing equipment is yours.** Fermenters, brewhouse, kegs — those are personal property, they leave with you. The TI covers *structural* improvements: drains, ventilation, walls, flooring, electrical upgrades.
- **The catch is the lease term.** A landlord putting $30-50K into your buildout will want a longer lease — 5 to 10 years — to recoup that investment. Some leases include a clawback clause: if you leave in year 2, you owe back a prorated portion of the TI.

So the negotiation is essentially: his money now in exchange for your commitment to stay.

**One non-negotiable before you sign:** Get a commercial real estate attorney to review the lease. $500–1,500 one-time cost. They'll catch things you won't — personal guarantee language, clawback terms, who's responsible for structural repairs, what happens if you want to sell the business. This is not where you save money.

And yeah — Sarah doing catering means she's worked in commercial kitchens, she'll know immediately what's dead and what's salvageable in there. Get her in with a checklist: hoods, fire suppression certification, walk-in compressor, ovens, three-compartment sink. If the big-ticket stuff works, your buildout math changes dramatically.

content.text:
That fear is the right fear to have. Anyone putting their whole savings into a business who *isn't* scared is the one I'd worry about.

So don't put all of it in.

Seriously. The number one killer of small food and beverage businesses isn't a bad concept — it's running out of cash in month four because everything cost more than expected and revenue took longer than projected. An $80K cushion that becomes a $0 cushion on opening day is a business running on fumes before it's sold its first pint.

**Protect yourselves:**

- **TI allowance from the landlord** takes $30-50K of buildout off your plate — that's his money, not yours
- **Northern Initiatives** (SBA microloan, right there in northern Michigan) can bridge $20-30K at reasonable rates for exactly this kind of startup
- **Sarah's catering keeps running** during buildout and early months. That's income. Don't shut it down the day you sign a lease.
- **Phase the spending.** Open lean — nano system, limited taps, simple menu. Let revenue fund the next stage. Your first six months don't need to look like your second year.

If you do it right, you open with maybe $40-50K of your own money in, a microloan covering a chunk, the landlord's TI covering buildout, Sarah still bringing in catering income, and **$30K still in the bank** for the surprises you can't predict yet. That's not reckless. That's a real plan.

The SBDC in Traverse City will help you model all of this out — cash flow projections, break-even analysis, seasonal revenue modeling. Free. That's the call to make after the kitchen walkthrough.

You're not betting everything. You're investing strategically and keeping a safety net. There's a big difference.
